ggplot2 Themes: Fundamentals, Structure & Custom Creation

A Comprehensive Guide with Working Examples

Cheng Peng

West Chester University

1 Theme Fundamentals

ggplot2’s theming system is a comprehensive framework that controls every non-data visual aspect of your plots. Themes handle backgrounds, grids, text formatting, legends, and faceting strips—essentially everything that makes your plot look polished and publication-ready.

Key Concept: Themes in ggplot2 follow a strict hierarchical inheritance structure. When you modify a theme element, you’re overriding the default values from the complete theme you started with.

1.1 Complete vs. Incomplete Themes

A complete theme specifies every visual property of a plot. These are designed to be standalone.

Examples:

  • theme_grey() (default)
  • theme_bw()
  • theme_minimal()
  • theme_classic()
  • theme_void()

Behavior: When you add + theme(...) to a complete theme, ggplot merges your changes with the existing complete specification.

An incomplete theme (created with theme()) specifies only some properties, leaving others to be inherited from the current complete theme.

Example:

my_incomplete <- theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45))

The following example contains workable code:

# Default theme (complete)
p_default <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Default theme_grey()")

# Built-in complete theme
p_bw <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "theme_bw()")

# Incomplete theme modification
p_modified <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "Modified theme_minimal()")

# Display comparison
(p_default + p_bw) / p_modified

The following two operators %+replace% and + are used in building complete and incomplete themes in ggplot().

1.2 %+replace% vs +

%+replace% – Use when you are building a completely custom theme from scratch (e.g., starting with theme() or theme_minimal() and wanting to replace entire element structures rather than merging).

+ – Use for incremental changes to an existing theme (e.g., starting with theme_bw() then modifying only axis text size).

Example: Using %+replace% (complete custom theme)

#library(ggplot2)

my_custom_theme <- theme_minimal() %+replace%
  theme(
    axis.title = element_text(color = "red", size = 20),
    axis.text = element_text(color = "blue", size = 12),
    # explicitly not inheriting from previous theme defaults
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "lightgray")
  )

Here, %+replace% replaces the entire axis.title, axis.text, and panel.background specifications instead of merging with the existing theme_minimal() defaults.

Example: Using + (incremental modification)

modified_theme <- theme_bw() +
  theme(
    axis.title.x = element_text(color = "red"),  # keeps other axis.title.y default
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  )

Here, only the specified changes are added; all other theme elements from theme_bw() remain unchanged.

Best Practices

Scenario Recommended Operator
Start with complete theme, tweak a few things +
Build completely custom theme from scratch theme_void() %+replace% theme(...)
Modify incomplete theme Almost always + (never %+replace% unless you know you want to purge defaults)
Remove a specific element set %+replace% with element_blank()
Pro Tip: Use %+replace% when creating a complete custom theme from scratch. Use + for incremental modifications to an existing theme.

2 The Hierarchy of GGPlot Elements

The ggplot and theme have a complex structure. Two hierarchical structures are useful in design and debugging. The component hierarchy diagram answers “what parts exist?” for customization, while the pipeline diagram answers “what happens when?” for understanding rendering order.

2.1 Processing pipeline

The rendering pipeline diagram (or grammar of graphics evaluation order). It outlines the dynamic processing flow, sequencing from geoms to aesthetics, scales, coordinate systems, facets, statistical transforms, and finally themes.

1. Geoms (Highest Priority): Layer-specific visual elements that override everything below

geom_point(color = "red", size = 3)           # Specific to this layer
geom_line(linetype = "dashed")                # Overrides any global settings

2. Aesthetics Mapping (aes()): Mapped variables override constants; scales transform these

aes(x = mpg, y = wt, color = cyl)             # Mapped to data
aes(x = mpg, y = wt, color = "blue")          # Treated as constant, NOT mapping

3. Scales: Transform and guide the visual representation of mapped aesthetics

scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red")  # Overrides default color scale
scale_x_log10()                                   # Transforms axis

4. Coordinate Systems (coord_xx()): Override axis and coordinate properties set by scales

coord_flip()                      # Swaps x and y axes
coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 10))  # Zooms (doesn't drop data)

5. Facets (facet_xx()): Split data into subplots; each facet inherits from global settings but can have its own scale

facet_wrap(~cyl, scales = "free")   # Different axis scales per facet

6. Statistical Transformations (stat_xx()): Override default statistical calculations

stat_smooth(method = "lm")           # Uses linear model instead of loess
stat_bin(bins = 30)                  # Overrides default binning

7. Themes (Lowest Priority): Control non-data ink: backgrounds, grids, text, legends (not data representation)

theme_bw() +                          # Complete theme
  theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 10))  # Specific element

2.2 ggplot2 Component hierarchy

The component hierarchy tree (or plot anatomy diagram). It illustrates the static, nested structure of a plot, showing how elements like the panel, axes, legend, strip, and titles branch into specific sub-components such as backgrounds, borders, grids, ticks, and text.

 
 plot
 |
 ├── panel
 │   ├── background
 │   ├── border
 │   ├── grid
 │   │   ├── major
 │   │   └── minor
 │   └── spacing
 ├── axis
 │   ├── title (x, y, top, right)
 │   ├── text (x, y, top, right)
 │   ├── ticks (x, y, top, right)
 │   └── line (x, y, top, right)
 ├── legend
 │   ├── background
 │   ├── title
 │   ├── text
 │   └── key
 ├── strip
 │   ├── background
 │   └── text
 └── plot
     ├── title
     ├── subtitle
     ├── caption
     └── tag

3 The Four Element Functions

The four element functions in ggplot2 are element_blank(), element_rect(), element_line(), and element_text() . These functions are the building blocks of the ggplot2 theme system, used to control the appearance of all non-data components of a plot .

  • element_blank() – Draws nothing and allocates no space; effectively removes a theme element .

  • element_rect() – Used for rectangular areas such as plot backgrounds, panel borders, and legend backgrounds .

  • element_line() – Used for lines including axis lines, grid lines, and tick marks .

  • element_text() – Used for text elements like plot titles, axis labels, legend text, and strip text .

Each function accepts specific parameters. For instance, element_text() supports family, face, colour, size, hjust, vjust, angle, and lineheight , while element_rect() accepts fill, colour, linewidth, and linetype.

Additional element types exist, such as element_polygon() and element_point(), but are not widely used .

# Create a base plot for demonstration
base_plot <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7)

# 1. element_text() - for all text elements
p_text <- base_plot +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(
      family = "serif",
      face = "bold.italic",
      colour = "#6E3061",
      size = 18,
      hjust = 0.5
    ),
    axis.title = element_text(
      face = "bold",
      colour = "grey30",
      size = 12
    ),
    axis.text = element_text(
      colour = "grey40",
      size = 10
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "Custom element_text() Example")

# 2. element_line() - for lines and borders
p_line <- base_plot +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 0.5,
      linetype = "dashed"
    ),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(
      colour = "#c49bb8",
      linewidth = 0.2,
      linetype = "dotted"
    ),
    axis.line = element_line(
      colour = "black",
      linewidth = 0.8
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "Custom element_line() Example")

# 3. element_rect() - for rectangles and backgrounds
p_rect <- base_plot +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(
      fill = "#f4edf3",
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 1
    ),
    plot.background = element_rect(
      fill = "#fdfafd",
      colour = NA
    ),
    legend.background = element_rect(
      fill = "white",
      colour = "#d4c0d1"
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "Custom element_rect() Example")

# 4. element_blank() - to remove elements
p_blank <- base_plot +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  labs(title = "element_blank() Example")

# Display all four
(p_text + p_line) / (p_rect + p_blank)

3.1 element_text() Parameter Reference

# Demonstrate key element_text parameters
demo_data <- data.frame(
  category = LETTERS[1:5],
  value = c(23, 45, 32, 56, 41)
)

ggplot(demo_data, aes(category, value)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#6E3061", alpha = 0.8) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    # Title with multiple parameters
    plot.title = element_text(
      family = "sans",
      face = "bold",
      colour = "#6E3061",
      size = 16,
      hjust = 0.5,
      margin = margin(b = 15)
    ),
    # Subtitle
    plot.subtitle = element_text(
      size = 12,
      colour = "grey40",
      hjust = 0.5
    ),
    # Axis titles
    axis.title.x = element_text(
      face = "italic",
      colour = "grey30",
      margin = margin(t = 10)
    ),
    axis.title.y = element_text(
      angle = 90,
      face = "italic",
      colour = "grey30"
    ),
    # Rotated axis text
    axis.text.x = element_text(
      angle = 45,
      hjust = 1,
      colour = "grey40"
    ),
    # Caption
    plot.caption = element_text(
      size = 8,
      colour = "grey60",
      hjust = 1,
      margin = margin(t = 10)
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Complete element_text() Demonstration",
    subtitle = "Showcasing all major text parameters",
    x = "Category",
    y = "Value",
    caption = "Source: Demo Data"
  )

Some of the graphical parameters used in the above plots are summarizedin the following table:

Parameter Description Example Values
family Font family name “sans”, “serif”, “mono”
face Font face/style “plain”, “italic”, “bold”, “bold.italic”
colour Text color “#6E3061”, “grey40”, “steelblue”
size Font size in pts 12, rel(0.9), rel(1.2)
hjust Horizontal justification 0 (left), 0.5 (center), 1 (right)
vjust Vertical justification 0 (bottom), 0.5 (middle), 1 (top)
angle Rotation angle 0, 45, 90
lineheight Line height multiplier 1, 1.5
margin Margin around text margin(t=10, r=5, b=10, l=5)

3.2 element_rect() Parameter Reference

element_rect() controls the appearance of rectangular elements: backgrounds (plot, panel, legend, strip), borders, and keys.

# Create a dedicated rectangle demonstration
rect_demo <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3)

# 1. fill: Interior color of rectangles
p_fill <- rect_demo +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    # Panel background fill
    panel.background = element_rect(
      fill = "#f4edf3",        # Light purple tint
      colour = NA               # No border
    ),
    # Plot background fill
    plot.background = element_rect(
      fill = "#fdfafd",        # Very light purple
      colour = NA
    ),
    # Legend key fill
    legend.key = element_rect(
      fill = "#faf5f9",        # Match panel
      colour = NA
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "fill: Custom background colors")

# 2. colour & linewidth: Borders on rectangles
p_border <- rect_demo +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    # Panel border with color and width
    panel.border = element_rect(
      fill = NA,                # Transparent interior
      colour = "#6E3061",      # Purple border
      linewidth = 1.5          # Thick border
    ),
    # Legend background with border
    legend.background = element_rect(
      fill = "white",
      colour = "#4a2040",      # Dark purple border
      linewidth = 0.5
    ),
    # Strip background with fill AND border
    strip.background = element_rect(
      fill = "#6E3061",        # Purple fill
      colour = "#4a2040",      # Darker border
      linewidth = 0.5
    )
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ gear) +
  labs(title = "colour + linewidth: Borders on panel, legend, strip")

# 3. linetype: Border pattern for rectangles
p_linetype <- rect_demo +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.border = element_rect(
      fill = NA,
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 1.2,
      linetype = "dashed"      # Dashed border
    ),
    legend.background = element_rect(
      fill = "white",
      colour = "#c49bb8",
      linewidth = 0.8,
      linetype = "dotted"      # Dotted border
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "linetype: 'dashed' panel, 'dotted' legend")

# 4. Multiple layered rectangles
p_layered <- rect_demo +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    # Outer plot background
    plot.background = element_rect(
      fill = "#e8dce5",        # Medium purple
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 1
    ),
    # Inner panel background
    panel.background = element_rect(
      fill = "white",
      colour = "#c49bb8",
      linewidth = 0.5,
      linetype = "dashed"
    ),
    # Legend sits on top
    legend.background = element_rect(
      fill = alpha("white", 0.9),  # Semi-transparent white
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 0.5
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "Layered: plot.bg > panel.bg > legend.bg")

# Display rectangle demonstrations
(p_fill + p_border) / (p_linetype + p_layered)

Some of the key graphical parameters used in the above example of element_redt() are summarized in the following table.

Parameter Type Default Description Example
fill character NULL Interior fill color
colour / color character NULL Border color
linewidth numeric NULL Border width in mm
linetype character/numeric NULL Border line pattern
inherit.blank logical FALSE Inherit blank from parent?
Key Pattern: Use fill = NA when you want a transparent interior with only a visible border. Use colour = NA when you want a filled rectangle with no border.

3.3 element_line() Parameter Reference

element_line() controls the appearance of all line elements: grid lines, axis lines, tick marks, panel borders, and more.

Syntax

element_line(
  colour = NULL,       # Line color (color also accepted)
  linewidth = NULL,    # Line width in mm (replaces deprecated 'size')
  linetype = NULL,     # Line type: "solid", "dashed", "dotted", "dotdash", 
                       # "longdash", "twodash", "blank"
  lineend = NULL,      # Line end style: "round", "butt", "square"
  arrow = NULL,        # Add arrow to line end (use arrow() function)
  inherit.blank = FALSE # Should blank lines inherit from parents?
)

Example

# Create a dedicated line demonstration
line_demo <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7, colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

# 1. linetype: Different line patterns
p_linetype <- line_demo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 0.6,
      linetype = "dashed"    # Try: solid, dashed, dotted, dotdash, longdash, twodash
    ),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(
      colour = "#c49bb8",
      linewidth = 0.3,
      linetype = "dotted"
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "linetype: 'dashed' major,\n 'dotted' minor")

# 2. linewidth: Controlling line thickness
p_linewidth <- line_demo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 1.2,       # Thicker lines
      linetype = "solid"
    ),
    axis.line = element_line(
      colour = "black",
      linewidth = 0.8        # Medium axis lines
    ),
    axis.ticks = element_line(
      colour = "grey50",
      linewidth = 0.3        # Thin ticks
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "linewidth: 1.2 (grid), \n 0.8 (axis), 0.3 (ticks)")

# 3. lineend: How line ends are rendered
p_lineend <- line_demo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 1.5,
      linetype = "dashed",
      lineend = "round"      # Try: "round", "butt", "square"
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "lineend: 'round' \n(visible on thick dashed lines)")

# 4. arrow: Adding arrows to lines
p_arrow <- line_demo +
  theme(
    axis.line.x = element_line(
      colour = "#6E3061",
      linewidth = 0.8,
      arrow = arrow(
        angle = 20,          # Arrowhead angle
        length = unit(0.3, "cm"),  # Arrowhead size
        ends = "last",       # "first", "last", "both"
        type = "closed"      # "open" or "closed"
      )
    )
  ) +
  labs(title = "arrow: Arrow on \n x-axis line end")

# Display line demonstrations
(p_linetype + p_linewidth) / (p_lineend + p_arrow)

Some graphical parameters used in the above example of element_line() is summarized in the following table.

Parameter Type Default Description Example
colour / color character NULL Line color
linewidth numeric NULL Line width in mm
linetype character/numeric NULL Line pattern
lineend character NULL Line cap style
arrow arrow object NULL Arrow specification
inherit.blank FALSE Inherit blank from parent? TRUE, FALSE
Note on linewidth vs size: In ggplot2 ≥ 3.4.0, linewidth replaced the deprecated size parameter for line elements. Always use linewidth for lines, size for points/text.

3.4 element_blank() Parameter Reference

element_blank() is the simplest yet most powerful element function. It removes a theme element entirely, as if it were never drawn.

Syntax

element_blank()

Working Example

base_for_blank <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3)

# 1. Remove all grid lines
p_no_grid <- base_for_blank +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "No Grid Lines")

# 2. Remove axis elements (minimalist look)
p_no_axis <- base_for_blank +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.line = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "No Axis Elements", subtitle = "Pure data display")

# 3. Remove legend and background elements
p_clean <- base_for_blank +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",         # Remove entire legend
    legend.background = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_blank(),
    plot.background = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "Clean Slate: No Legend or Backgrounds")

# 4. Remove strip elements (for faceted plots)
p_no_strips <- base_for_blank +
  facet_wrap(~ gear) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.background = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "No Strip Headers", subtitle = "Facet identity hidden")

# Display blank demonstrations
(p_no_grid + p_no_axis) / (p_clean + p_no_strips)

element_blank() Common Usage Table

Theme Element to Blank Effect Common Use Case
panel.grid.major Removes major grid lines Clean, minimalist plots
panel.grid.minor Removes minor grid lines Reduce visual clutter
panel.grid Removes ALL grid lines Complete grid removal
axis.ticks Removes tick marks Modern, streamlined look
axis.text Removes tick labels When labels aren’t needed
axis.title Removes axis titles When axes are self-explanatory
axis.line Removes axis lines Combine with void theme
legend.title Removes legend title When legend is self-explanatory
legend.key Removes legend key background Transparent legend symbols
strip.background Removes facet strip background Minimalist faceting
strip.text Removes facet labels Hide facet identity
panel.background Removes panel fill Transparent plotting area
plot.background Removes plot fill Transparent overall background
panel.border Removes panel border No framing rectangle
Important: element_blank() completely removes an element. For legends, use legend.position = “none” instead of blanking all legend sub-elements individually.

3.5 Overall Summary: Key Graphical Parameters

Below is a comprehensive cross-reference table of all key graphical parameters used across the four element functions. Use this as your quick reference when building custom themes.

Parameter Type Used In Description & Common Values
family character element_text() Font family name. Common: “sans”, “serif”, “mono”, “Arial”, “Times”
face character element_text() Font style: “plain”, “italic”, “bold”, “bold.italic”
colour / color character element_text()
element_line()
element_rect() 		Color specification. Accepts: hex codes (“#6E3061”), named colors (“steelblue”), NA (transparent/no color)
size numeric element_text() Font size in points. Use rel() for scaling: 12, rel(0.9), rel(1.2)
linewidth numeric element_line()
element_rect() 		Line/border width in mm. Common: 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0
linetype character / numeric element_line()
element_rect() 		Line pattern. Character: “solid”, “dashed”, “dotted”, “dotdash”, “longdash”, “twodash”, “blank”. Numeric: 1–6
fill character element_rect() Interior fill color for rectangles. Common: “white”, “grey95”, “#f4edf3”, NA (transparent)
hjust numeric (0–1) element_text() Horizontal justification: 0 (left), 0.5 (center), 1 (right)
vjust numeric (0–1) element_text() Vertical justification: 0 (bottom), 0.5 (middle), 1 (top)
angle numeric element_text() Rotation angle in degrees: 0 (horizontal), 45, 90 (vertical), -45, 270
lineheight numeric element_text() Line spacing multiplier: 1 (normal), 1.5 (loose), 0.8 (tight)
margin margin object element_text() Space around text: margin(t=10, r=5, b=10, l=5). t=top, r=right, b=bottom, l=left
lineend character element_line() Line cap style: “round”, “butt”, “square”. Most visible on thick lines
arrow arrow object element_line() Arrow specification: arrow(angle=20, length=unit(0.3,“cm”), ends=“last”, type=“closed”)
debug logical element_text() Draw debug rectangle: TRUE, FALSE (default). Useful for layout debugging
inherit.blank logical element_line()
element_rect() 		Inherit blank from parent element: TRUE, FALSE (default). Advanced usage only
Quick Tip: The colour parameter accepts both British (colour) and American (color) spellings. They are completely interchangeable in ggplot2.

3.6 element_rect() Reference

Core Parameters for Borders and Backgrounds

fill : The fill color for the rectangular area (e.g., the panel or plot background). Accepts color names, hex codes, or RGB values. Use fill_alpha() to set transparency .

colour / color : The border line color. color is an alias for colour .

linewidth : The border size, measured in mm . (Note: the older size parameter is deprecated for this purpose).

linetype : The border line type. Can be specified as an integer (0-8), a name (“blank”, “solid”, “dashed”, “dotted”, “dotdash”, “longdash”, “twodash”), or a hexadecimal string (e.g., “44”) .

Additional Styling Parameters linejoin : Controls how corners of the border are rendered. Options include “round”, “mitre”, or “bevel” .

inherit.blank : Logical value. If TRUE and a parent element is element_blank(), this element will also be blank. If FALSE, it ignores blank parents .

Deprecated Parameter size : This parameter is deprecated. Use linewidth for border thickness or fontsize for text-related sizing instead .

Usage Tip: This function is most commonly used within theme() to modify elements such as panel.background, plot.background, or legend.background .

4 Step-by-Step Custom Theme Creation

This section use working examples to demonstrate the steps for create a custom theme.

4.1 Step 1: Create the Theme Function Skeleton

# Step 1: Define the theme function
theme_custom <- function(base_size = 12, base_family = "") {
  # Start with a complete theme and replace everything
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # We'll fill this in through the following steps
    )
}

# Test the skeleton
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_custom() +
  labs(title = "Step 1: Theme Skeleton")

Important: Always start with base_size and base_family as parameters. This makes your theme reusable and scalable.

4.2 Step 2: Configure Plot and Panel Backgrounds

theme_custom <- function(base_size = 12, base_family = "") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # Plot background (behind everything)
      plot.background = element_rect(
        fill = "white",
        colour = NA,
        linewidth = 0
      ),
      # Panel background (the plotting area)
      panel.background = element_rect(
        fill = "grey95",
        colour = NA
      ),
      # Panel border
      panel.border = element_rect(
        fill = NA,
        colour = "grey80",
        linewidth = 0.5
      )
    )
}

# Test the backgrounds
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_custom() +
  labs(title = "Step 2: Custom Backgrounds",
       subtitle = "Grey panel with border")

4.3 Step 3: Define Grid Lines

theme_custom <- function(base_size = 12, base_family = "") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA),
      panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = "grey80", linewidth = 0.5),
      
      # Major grid lines
      panel.grid.major = element_line(
        colour = "grey85",
        linewidth = 0.3,
        linetype = "dashed"
      ),
      # Minor grid lines
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(
        colour = "grey90",
        linewidth = 0.15,
        linetype = "dotted"
      )
    )
}

# Test grid lines
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_custom() +
  labs(title = "Step 3: Custom Grid Lines",
       subtitle = "Dashed major grid, dotted minor grid")

4.4 Step 4: Configure Axis Elements

theme_custom <- function(base_size = 12, base_family = "") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA),
      panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = "grey80", linewidth = 0.5),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey85", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dashed"),
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(colour = "grey90", linewidth = 0.15, linetype = "dotted"),
      
      # Axis lines
      axis.line = element_line(colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
      # Axis ticks
      axis.ticks = element_line(colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.3),
      axis.ticks.length = unit(2, "mm"),
      # Axis titles
      axis.title = element_text(
        size = rel(1.1),
        face = "bold",
        colour = "grey30"
      ),
      # Axis text (tick labels)
      axis.text = element_text(
        size = rel(0.9),
        colour = "grey40"
      ),
      # Individual axis title margins
      axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10)),
      axis.title.y = element_text(angle = 90, margin = margin(r = 10))
    )
}

# Test axis configuration
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_custom(base_size = 14) +
  labs(title = "Step 4: Custom Axis Elements",
       x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
       y = "Miles per Gallon")

4.5 Step 5: Configure the Legend

theme_custom <- function(base_size = 12, base_family = "") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA),
      panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = "grey80", linewidth = 0.5),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey85", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dashed"),
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(colour = "grey90", linewidth = 0.15, linetype = "dotted"),
      axis.line = element_line(colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
      axis.ticks = element_line(colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.3),
      axis.ticks.length = unit(2, "mm"),
      axis.title = element_text(size = rel(1.1), face = "bold", colour = "grey30"),
      axis.text = element_text(size = rel(0.9), colour = "grey40"),
      axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10)),
      axis.title.y = element_text(angle = 90, margin = margin(r = 10)),
      
      # Legend background
      legend.background = element_rect(
        fill = "white",
        colour = "grey80",
        linewidth = 0.3
      ),
      # Legend title
      legend.title = element_text(
        size = rel(0.95),
        face = "bold",
        colour = "grey30"
      ),
      # Legend text
      legend.text = element_text(
        size = rel(0.85),
        colour = "grey40"
      ),
      # Legend key (the symbol area)
      legend.key = element_rect(
        fill = "white",
        colour = NA
      ),
      # Legend position and spacing
      legend.position = "bottom",
      legend.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5),
      legend.spacing = unit(5, "mm")
    )
}

# Test legend configuration
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl), shape = factor(gear))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_custom(base_size = 14) +
  labs(title = "Step 5: Custom Legend",
       colour = "Cylinders",
       shape = "Gears")

4.6 Step 6: Define Plot Titles and Labels

theme_custom <- function(base_size = 12, base_family = "") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # Previous steps...
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA),
      panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = "grey80", linewidth = 0.5),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey85", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dashed"),
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(colour = "grey90", linewidth = 0.15, linetype = "dotted"),
      axis.line = element_line(colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
      axis.ticks = element_line(colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.3),
      axis.ticks.length = unit(2, "mm"),
      axis.title = element_text(size = rel(1.1), face = "bold", colour = "grey30"),
      axis.text = element_text(size = rel(0.9), colour = "grey40"),
      axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10)),
      axis.title.y = element_text(angle = 90, margin = margin(r = 10)),
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = "grey80", linewidth = 0.3),
      legend.title = element_text(size = rel(0.95), face = "bold", colour = "grey30"),
      legend.text = element_text(size = rel(0.85), colour = "grey40"),
      legend.key = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      
      # Plot title
      plot.title = element_text(
        size = rel(1.4),
        face = "bold",
        colour = "#6E3061",
        hjust = 0.5,
        margin = margin(b = 10)
      ),
      # Subtitle
      plot.subtitle = element_text(
        size = rel(1.1),
        colour = "grey40",
        hjust = 0.5,
        margin = margin(b = 15)
      ),
      # Caption
      plot.caption = element_text(
        size = rel(0.8),
        colour = "grey60",
        hjust = 1,
        margin = margin(t = 10)
      ),
      # Tag (for labeling subplots)
      plot.tag = element_text(
        size = rel(1.2),
        face = "bold",
        colour = "#6E3061"
      ),
      # Overall plot margins
      plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)
    )
}

# Test title configuration
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_custom(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    title = "Step 6: Custom Titles & Labels",
    subtitle = "Complete text hierarchy with custom styling",
    caption = "Source: mtcars dataset",
    colour = "Cylinders",
    tag = "A"
  )

4.7 Step 7: Configure Facet Strips

theme_custom <- function(base_size = 12, base_family = "") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # All previous steps...
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA),
      panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = "grey80", linewidth = 0.5),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey85", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dashed"),
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(colour = "grey90", linewidth = 0.15, linetype = "dotted"),
      axis.line = element_line(colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
      axis.ticks = element_line(colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.3),
      axis.ticks.length = unit(2, "mm"),
      axis.title = element_text(size = rel(1.1), face = "bold", colour = "grey30"),
      axis.text = element_text(size = rel(0.9), colour = "grey40"),
      axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10)),
      axis.title.y = element_text(angle = 90, margin = margin(r = 10)),
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = "grey80", linewidth = 0.3),
      legend.title = element_text(size = rel(0.95), face = "bold", colour = "grey30"),
      legend.text = element_text(size = rel(0.85), colour = "grey40"),
      legend.key = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      plot.title = element_text(size = rel(1.4), face = "bold", colour = "#6E3061", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 10)),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = rel(1.1), colour = "grey40", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 15)),
      plot.caption = element_text(size = rel(0.8), colour = "grey60", hjust = 1, margin = margin(t = 10)),
      plot.tag = element_text(size = rel(1.2), face = "bold", colour = "#6E3061"),
      plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20),
      
      # Strip background (facet headers)
      strip.background = element_rect(
        fill = "#6E3061",
        colour = "#4a2040",
        linewidth = 0.3
      ),
      # Strip text
      strip.text = element_text(
        size = rel(1.1),
        face = "bold",
        colour = "white"
      ),
      # Strip placement
      strip.placement = "outside"
    )
}

# Test facet configuration
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#6E3061") +
  facet_wrap(~ gear, nrow = 2) +
  theme_custom(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    title = "Step 7: Custom Facet Strips",
    subtitle = "Styled strip headers with custom colors",
    caption = "Source: mtcars dataset"
  )

4.8 Putting It All Together: A Complete Theme Example

Now that we’ve covered all four element functions and their parameters, here’s a complete theme that demonstrates proper usage of every function:

theme_master_demo <- function(base_size = 14, base_family = "") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # === element_rect() usage ===
      plot.background = element_rect(
        fill = "#fdfafd",
        colour = NA
      ),
      panel.background = element_rect(
        fill = "white",
        colour = NA
      ),
      panel.border = element_rect(
        fill = NA,
        colour = "#6E3061",
        linewidth = 0.8
      ),
      legend.background = element_rect(
        fill = "white",
        colour = "#d4c0d1",
        linewidth = 0.3
      ),
      legend.key = element_rect(
        fill = "white",
        colour = NA
      ),
      strip.background = element_rect(
        fill = "#6E3061",
        colour = "#4a2040",
        linewidth = 0.3
      ),
      
      # === element_line() usage ===
      panel.grid.major = element_line(
        colour = "grey90",
        linewidth = 0.3,
        linetype = "solid"
      ),
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(
        colour = "grey95",
        linewidth = 0.15,
        linetype = "dotted"
      ),
      axis.line = element_line(
        colour = "#6E3061",
        linewidth = 0.6
      ),
      axis.ticks = element_line(
        colour = "grey60",
        linewidth = 0.4
      ),
      
      # === element_text() usage ===
      plot.title = element_text(
        family = base_family,
        face = "bold",
        colour = "#6E3061",
        size = rel(1.4),
        hjust = 0.5,
        margin = margin(b = 12)
      ),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(
        colour = "grey40",
        size = rel(1.1),
        hjust = 0.5,
        margin = margin(b = 15)
      ),
      plot.caption = element_text(
        colour = "grey50",
        size = rel(0.8),
        hjust = 1,
        margin = margin(t = 12)
      ),
      plot.tag = element_text(
        face = "bold",
        colour = "#6E3061",
        size = rel(1.2)
      ),
      axis.title = element_text(
        face = "bold",
        colour = "grey30",
        size = rel(1.05)
      ),
      axis.text = element_text(
        colour = "grey40",
        size = rel(0.9)
      ),
      legend.title = element_text(
        face = "bold",
        colour = "grey30",
        size = rel(0.95)
      ),
      legend.text = element_text(
        colour = "grey40",
        size = rel(0.85)
      ),
      strip.text = element_text(
        face = "bold",
        colour = "white",
        size = rel(1.1)
      ),
      
      # === element_blank() usage ===
      # (we keep these, but they would be element_blank() if removed)
      
      # === Spacing ===
      axis.ticks.length = unit(2.5, "mm"),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      legend.margin = margin(8, 8, 8, 8),
      plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20),
      strip.placement = "outside"
    )
}
# Apply the complete theme to a comprehensive plot
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl), size = hp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.1, linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ gear, nrow = 2) +
  theme_master_demo(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    title = "Complete Theme: All Four Element Functions",
    subtitle = "Demonstrating element_text(), element_line(),\n element_rect(), and element_blank()",
    caption = "Data: mtcars | Theme: theme_master_demo()",
    x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y = "Miles per Gallon",
    colour = "Cylinders",
    size = "Horsepower",
    tag = "Fig. 1"
  )

5 Complete Custom Theme Examples

Recall that the primary purpose of theme() is to separate data representation from stylistic decoration, ensuring that visual clutter does not distract from the underlying insights. It enables consistency across multiple plots, supports branding or journal formatting, and adheres to principles like maximizing the data-ink ratio by removing unnecessary grid lines or backgrounds. Ultimately, mastering theme() empowers you to transform a default ggplot into a clear, professional, and customized visualization that effectively communicates your data’s story.

This section offers a few complete examples for different purposes.

5.1 Publication-Ready Theme

Because different journals require different visual styles, theme() enables you to generate plots that are consistent with the specific formatting requirements of each publication.

theme_publication <- function(base_size = 14, base_family = "serif") {
  theme_bw(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # Clean background
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white"),
      panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      
      # Axis formatting
      axis.line = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks = element_line(colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
      axis.ticks.length = unit(2, "mm"),
      axis.text = element_text(size = rel(0.9), colour = "black"),
      axis.title = element_text(size = rel(1.1), face = "bold"),
      
      # Legend
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = "black", linewidth = 0.3),
      legend.key = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      
      # Titles
      plot.title = element_text(size = rel(1.3), face = "bold", hjust = 0),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = rel(1), hjust = 0),
      plot.caption = element_text(size = rel(0.8), hjust = 1),
      plot.margin = margin(15, 15, 15, 15),
      
      # Strips
      strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey90", colour = "black"),
      strip.text = element_text(size = rel(1), face = "bold")
    )
}

# Demonstrate publication theme
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.15) +
  facet_wrap(~ gear) +
  theme_publication(base_size = 14, base_family = "serif") +
  labs(
    title = "Fuel Efficiency by Weight and Cylinders",
    subtitle = "Linear regression with 95% confidence intervals",
    caption = "Data: 1974 Motor Trend US magazine",
    x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y = "Miles per Gallon",
    colour = "Cylinders"
  )

Publication Tip: Always use base_family = “serif” for academic publications. Most journals prefer serif fonts. Set base_size = 14 or higher for readability.

5.2 Dashboard Minimal Theme

Dashboard environments demand visual clarity and fast interpretation. Unlike publication-ready plots that may tolerate decorative elements, dashboards benefit from a restrained, minimalist approach. The Dashboard Minimal Theme strips away unnecessary chart junk—including background fills, minor grid lines, and excess borders—to focus entirely on the data. This clean aesthetic reduces cognitive load, ensures consistency across multiple panels, and allows end users to extract insights at a glance, making it the optimal choice for interactive and real-time dashboards.

theme_dashboard <- function(base_size = 12, base_family = "") {
  theme_void(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # Minimal grid
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      
      # Simple axis lines
      axis.line = element_line(colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3),
      axis.ticks = element_line(colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3),
      axis.text = element_text(size = rel(0.9), colour = "grey40", margin = margin(5)),
      axis.title = element_text(size = rel(0.9), face = "bold", colour = "grey30"),
      
      # Floating legend
      legend.position = c(0.9, 0.9),
      legend.justification = c(1, 1),
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = alpha("white", 0.8), colour = NA),
      legend.key = element_blank(),
      
      # Compact titles
      plot.title = element_text(size = rel(1.2), face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
      plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
    )
}

# Demonstrate dashboard theme
ggplot(mtcars, aes(factor(cyl), mpg, fill = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.colour = "#6E3061", outlier.size = 2) +
  theme_dashboard(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    title = "MPG Distribution by Cylinder Count",
    x = "Cylinders",
    y = "MPG",
    fill = "Cylinders"
  ) #+

 # scale_fill_brewer
Dashboard Tip: Use theme_void() as the base for maximum control. Floating legends save space. Keep margins compact for dashboard layouts.

5.3 Dark Presentation Theme

A well-built plot answers a question, but a well-themed plot commands attention and credibility. The “presentation theme” in ggplot2 encompasses all stylistic choices that are independent of the underlying data—from panel backgrounds and grid lines to legend placement and title fonts. Mastering the theme() function allows you to adapt any graphic for its final medium, ensuring consistency across a report, adherence to journal guidelines, or alignment with a brand’s visual identity.

theme_dark_presentation <- function(base_size = 16, base_family = "") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # Dark background
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "grey20", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "grey25", colour = NA),
      
      # Grid lines visible on dark
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3),
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(colour = "grey35", linewidth = 0.15),
      
      # Light text for contrast
      axis.text = element_text(colour = "grey90", size = rel(0.9)),
      axis.title = element_text(colour = "white", size = rel(1.1), face = "bold"),
      plot.title = element_text(colour = "white", size = rel(1.4), face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey80", size = rel(1), hjust = 0.5),
      plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey70", size = rel(0.7)),
      
      # Legend on dark
      legend.text = element_text(colour = "grey90"),
      legend.title = element_text(colour = "white", face = "bold"),
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = "grey30", colour = "grey40"),
      legend.key = element_rect(fill = "grey25", colour = NA),
      
      # Strip formatting
      strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey40", colour = "grey50"),
      strip.text = element_text(colour = "white", face = "bold")
    )
}

# Demonstrate dark theme
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl), size = hp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  theme_dark_presentation(base_size = 16) +
  labs(
    title = "Vehicle Performance Analysis",
    subtitle = "Weight vs. Fuel Efficiency by Cylinder Count",
    caption = "Bubble size indicates horsepower",
    x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y = "Miles per Gallon",
    colour = "Cylinders",
    size = "Horsepower"
  ) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1")

Accessibility Note: Ensure sufficient color contrast in dark themes. Use WebAIM’s Contrast Checker to verify WCAG 2.1 AA compliance (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text).

6 Theme Inheritance and Modification

Theme inheritance in ggplot2 follows a clear hierarchical structure: a complete theme provides default settings for all elements, and you can then modify individual components without disrupting the rest. This layered approach saves time and ensures consistency—you define global styles once, then make targeted overrides for titles, legends, or grid lines as needed.

6.1 %+replace% vs + Operator Revsited

As we have discuss these two operators earlier, the + operator modifies theme elements while preserving unspecified settings, whereas %+replace% completely replaces them—a critical distinction for avoiding unexpected overrides when customizing ggplot2 themes. Here is another example.

# Create a base custom theme
theme_my_base <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) %+replace%
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#6E3061")
    )
}

# COMPLETE REPLACEMENT: %+replace% removes ALL inherited elements
theme_complete_replace <- theme_my_base() %+replace%
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "red", linewidth = 1)
  )

# INCREMENTAL ADDITION: + keeps other elements, only adds/overrides
theme_incremental_add <- theme_my_base() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "blue", linewidth = 1)
  )

# Compare the results
p1 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_complete_replace +
  labs(title = "%+replace% : Only red grid remains")

p2 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_incremental_add +
  labs(title = "+ : Blue grid + bold title retained")

p1 + p2

Rule of Thumb: Use %+replace% when building a theme from scratch inside a function. Use + when making ad-hoc modifications to a plot.

6.2 Creating Theme Variants

# Base theme with parameterized color
theme_branded <- function(base_size = 12, brand_color = "#6E3061") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) %+replace%
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      axis.title = element_text(colour = brand_color, face = "bold"),
      plot.title = element_text(colour = brand_color, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
      strip.background = element_rect(fill = brand_color),
      strip.text = element_text(colour = "white", face = "bold")
    )
}

# Create variants with different brand colors
p_purple <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#6E3061", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ gear) +
  theme_branded(brand_color = "#6E3061") +
  labs(title = "Purple Brand (#6E3061)")

p_blue <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#2196F3", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ gear) +
  theme_branded(brand_color = "#2196F3") +
  labs(title = "Blue Brand (#2196F3)")

p_red <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#E74C3C", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ gear) +
  theme_branded(brand_color = "#E74C3C") +
  labs(title = "Red Brand (#E74C3C)")

# Display variants
p_purple / p_blue / p_red

7 Advanced Techniques

7.1 Responsive Theme with Scaling

theme_responsive <- function(base_size = 12, ratio = 1) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) %+replace%
    theme(
      # Scale elements based on ratio parameter
      axis.text = element_text(size = base_size * ratio * 0.8),
      axis.title = element_text(size = base_size * ratio * 0.9),
      plot.title = element_text(size = base_size * ratio * 1.2, face = "bold"),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(linewidth = 0.5 * ratio),
      plot.margin = margin(
        t = 10 * ratio,
        r = 10 * ratio,
        b = 10 * ratio,
        l = 10 * ratio
      )
    )
}

# Compare different scales
p_small <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_responsive(ratio = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Ratio = 0.7 (Small)")

p_normal <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_responsive(ratio = 1.0) +
  labs(title = "Ratio = 1.0 (Normal)")

p_large <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_responsive(ratio = 1.5) +
  labs(title = "Ratio = 1.5 (Large)")

p_small + p_normal + p_large

7.2 Conditional Theme Elements

theme_conditional <- function(base_size = 12, show_grid = TRUE, show_border = FALSE) {
  t <- theme_minimal(base_size = base_size)
  
  if (!show_grid) {
    t <- t %+replace% theme(
      panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    )
  }
  
  if (show_border) {
    t <- t %+replace% theme(
      panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = "#6E3061", linewidth = 1)
    )
  }
  
  return(t)
}

# Demonstrate conditional themes
p1 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_conditional(show_grid = TRUE, show_border = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Grid: ON | Border: OFF")

p2 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_conditional(show_grid = FALSE, show_border = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Grid: OFF | Border: OFF")

p3 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#6E3061") +
  theme_conditional(show_grid = FALSE, show_border = TRUE) +
  labs(title = "Grid: OFF | Border: ON")

p1 + p2 + p3

7.3 Global Theme Settings

# Save the current theme
old_theme <- theme_get()

# Set a custom theme globally
theme_set(
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#6E3061", hjust = 0.5),
      axis.title = element_text(face = "bold")
    )
)

# All subsequent plots will use this theme
p_global <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "This plot uses the global theme",
    subtitle = "No explicit theme() call needed"
  )

print(p_global)

# Restore the original theme
theme_set(old_theme)
Caution: theme_set() affects all subsequent plots in the R session. Always restore the original theme or use it in a controlled environment like a dedicated script.

7.4 Debugging and Testing Themes

# Create a theme for testing
my_test_theme <- theme_custom(base_size = 12)

# 1. Inspect individual elements
cat("Panel grid major setting:\n")
Panel grid major setting:
print(my_test_theme$panel.grid.major)
<ggplot2::element_line>
 @ colour       : chr "grey85"
 @ linewidth    : num 0.3
 @ linetype     : chr "dashed"
 @ lineend      : NULL
 @ linejoin     : NULL
 @ arrow        : logi FALSE
 @ arrow.fill   : chr "grey85"
 @ inherit.blank: logi FALSE
cat("\nPlot title setting:\n")

Plot title setting:
print(my_test_theme$plot.title)
<ggplot2::element_text>
 @ family       : NULL
 @ face         : chr "bold"
 @ italic       : chr NA
 @ fontweight   : num NA
 @ fontwidth    : num NA
 @ colour       : chr "#6E3061"
 @ size         : 'rel' num 1.4
 @ hjust        : num 0.5
 @ vjust        : NULL
 @ angle        : NULL
 @ lineheight   : NULL
 @ margin       : <ggplot2::margin> num [1:4] 0 0 10 0
 @ debug        : NULL
 @ inherit.blank: logi FALSE
# 2. Use calc_element() for computed values
cat("\nComputed panel background:\n")

Computed panel background:
print(calc_element("panel.background", my_test_theme))
<ggplot2::element_rect>
 @ fill         : chr "grey95"
 @ colour       : logi NA
 @ linewidth    : num 0.545
 @ linetype     : num 1
 @ linejoin     : chr "round"
 @ inherit.blank: logi FALSE
# 3. Check for missing elements compared to a complete theme
missing_elements <- setdiff(names(theme_grey()), names(my_test_theme))
if (length(missing_elements) > 0) {
  cat("\nMissing elements compared to theme_grey():\n")
  print(missing_elements)
} else {
  cat("\nNo missing elements found!\n")
}

No missing elements found!
# 4. Create a comprehensive test plot
test_all_features <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ gear, nrow = 2) +
  my_test_theme +
  labs(
    title = "Comprehensive Theme Test",
    subtitle = "Tests points, lines, facets, and legends",
    caption = "Verify all elements render correctly",
    colour = "Cylinders",
    tag = "A"
  )

print(test_all_features)

8 Best Practices Summary

#' Custom ggplot2 theme - Best Practice Example
#'
#' @param base_size Base font size in points (default: 12)
#' @param base_family Base font family (default: "")
#' @param accent_color Primary accent color for titles and highlights
#'
#' @return A complete ggplot2 theme object
#' @export
#'
#' @examples
#' library(ggplot2)
#' ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
#'   geom_point() +
#'   theme_best_practice(accent_color = "#6E3061")
theme_best_practice <- function(base_size = 12,
                                 base_family = "",
                                 accent_color = "#6E3061") {

  # Validate font availability
  if (base_family != "") {
    available_fonts <- systemfonts::system_fonts()$family
    if (!base_family %in% available_fonts) {
      warning("Font family '", base_family, "' not found. Using system default.")
      base_family <- ""
    }
  }

  # Build the theme
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size, base_family = base_family) %+replace%
    theme(
      # Use rel() for scalable sizes
      plot.title = element_text(
        size = rel(1.3),
        face = "bold",
        colour = accent_color
      ),
      axis.title = element_text(size = rel(1.1)),
      axis.text = element_text(size = rel(0.9)),
      # ... (remaining theme elements)
    )
}
Final Checklist for Production Themes:
  • ✅ Always provide base_size and base_family parameters
  • ✅ Use rel() for scalable font sizes
  • ✅ Handle missing fonts gracefully
  • ✅ Test with scatter, bar, facet, and multi-legend plots
  • ✅ Include complete Roxygen documentation
  • ✅ Verify WCAG 2.1 AA color contrast ratios
  • ✅ Test on both screen and print output

This R Markdown document provides:

Complete structure:

  • YAML header with table of contents, code folding, and syntax highlighting
  • Organized into clear sections matching the original flash card content
  • Embedded CSS for professional lecture note formatting

Working examples throughout:

  • Every concept demonstrated with executable R code
  • Side-by-side plot comparisons using patchwork
  • Step-by-step theme building from skeleton to complete theme
  • Three complete theme examples (Publication, Dashboard, Dark)

Visual design:

  • Purple #6E3061 accent color consistent with the original
  • Custom styled callout boxes (note, tip, warning)
  • Formatted tables for parameter references
  • Clean code blocks with syntax highlighting

Key features: - Floating table of contents - Numbered sections - Collapsible code blocks - Professional figure formatting - Complete session information at the end
